title: MVP: Multi-task Supervised Pre-training for Natural Language Generation

structured.summary: This paper proposes MVP, a multi-task supervised pre-training framework for natural language generation that leverages a large, diverse labeled corpus (MVPCorpus) and task-specific soft prompts to improve performance and generalization across 11 NLG tasks. MVP achieves state-of-the-art results on 13 out of 17 datasets, outperforms unsupervised and instruction-tuned baselines, and demonstrates strong zero-shot and unseen task generalization.

strengths.point: Comprehensive multi-task supervised pre-training with a large labeled corpus
strengths.evidence: Collected MVPCorpus with 77 datasets across 11 diverse NLG tasks totaling 32M examples, enabling effective multi-task learning.
MVP outperforms BART-large and Flan-T5 by significant margins, achieves SOTA on 13/17 datasets, and shows strong zero-shot and unseen task transfer.

strengths.point: Innovative use of task-specific soft prompts to mitigate multi-task interference
strengths.evidence: Task-specific prompts inserted at each Transformer layer improve performance over full fine-tuning and prefix-tuning baselines.

strengths.point: Unified text-to-text format with human-written task instructions
strengths.evidence: All tasks converted to text-to-text with prepended instructions, facilitating multi-task learning and zero-shot generalization.

strengths.point: Open release of data, models, prompts, and code
strengths.evidence: MVPCorpus, MVP models, task-specific prompts, and code are publicly released to support reproducibility and future research.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to recent instruction-tuned large language models
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation against InstructGPT, ChatGPT, or other instruction-tuned LLMs, weakening claims of superiority in zero-shot settings.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of task instruction design and multi-task interference
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation on instruction formulations or quantitative analysis of task interference beyond qualitative claims.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics and qualitative error analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation relies mainly on ROUGE/BLEU and limited human evaluation without comprehensive statistical significance or failure case analysis.
Baselines differ in model size and training data; details on dataset matching and compute parity are missing.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility concerns due to missing details
weaknesses.evidence: Data cleaning criteria, prompt tuning hyperparameters, and temperature-scaled task mixing methodology are insufficiently described.

weaknesses.point: No discussion on computational cost and efficiency
weaknesses.evidence: Training requires 32 GPUs and large batch sizes, but energy consumption, training time, and resource requirements are not reported.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation on multilingual or non-English tasks
weaknesses.evidence: MVPCorpus and experiments focus exclusively on English, limiting claims about broad applicability.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient clarity on task-specific prompt architecture
weaknesses.evidence: Details such as prompt length, embedding dimensions, and integration with the backbone model are not clearly explained.

weaknesses.point: No comparison to other parameter-efficient tuning methods
weaknesses.evidence: Only prefix-tuning is compared; adapters, LoRA, or other methods are not evaluated.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of potential overfitting to seen tasks
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis on how performance degrades with decreasing task similarity or overfitting risks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of discussion on related concurrent work
weaknesses.evidence: No comparison or contrast with recent multi-task supervised pre-training efforts like UnifiedSKG or T5-based models.

questions.question: How does MVP compare to instruction-tuned large language models like InstructGPT or ChatGPT in zero-shot and few-shot NLG tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding this comparison is critical to position MVP's contributions relative to state-of-the-art instruction-tuned LLMs.

questions.question: What is the impact of different task instruction formulations on multi-task learning performance?
questions.motivation: Since instructions guide the model, analyzing their design could optimize multi-task learning and zero-shot generalization.

questions.question: How does multi-task supervised pre-training affect task interference quantitatively, and can this be mitigated beyond soft prompts?
questions.motivation: Quantitative insights into interference can guide better multi-task training strategies and improve model robustness.

questions.question: What are the computational costs and efficiency trade-offs of MVP compared to unsupervised pre-training or instruction tuning?
questions.motivation: Resource requirements impact practical adoption, especially for researchers with limited compute.

questions.question: Can MVP be effectively extended to multilingual or cross-lingual NLG tasks?
questions.motivation: Extending MVP beyond English would increase its applicability and relevance in diverse linguistic contexts.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to recent instruction-tuned large language models. No evaluation against InstructGPT, ChatGPT, or other instruction-tuned LLMs, weakening claims of superiority in zero-shot settings
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient analysis of task instruction design and multi-task interference. No ablation on instruction formulations or quantitative analysis of task interference beyond qualitative claims
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation metrics and qualitative error analysis. Evaluation relies mainly on ROUGE/BLEU and limited human evaluation without comprehensive statistical significance or failure case analysis
critique_points.point_004: Unclear fairness in baseline comparisons. Baselines differ in model size and training data; details on dataset matching and compute parity are missing
critique_points.point_005: Reproducibility concerns due to missing details. Data cleaning criteria, prompt tuning hyperparameters, and temperature-scaled task mixing methodology are insufficiently described
critique_points.point_006: No discussion on computational cost and efficiency. Training requires 32 GPUs and large batch sizes, but energy consumption, training time, and resource requirements are not reported
critique_points.point_007: Limited evaluation on multilingual or non-English tasks. MVPCorpus and experiments focus exclusively on English, limiting claims about broad applicability
critique_points.point_008: Insufficient clarity on task-specific prompt architecture. Details such as prompt length, embedding dimensions, and integration with the backbone model are not clearly explained
critique_points.point_009: No comparison to other parameter-efficient tuning methods. Only prefix-tuning is compared; adapters, LoRA, or other methods are not evaluated
critique_points.point_010: No discussion of potential overfitting to seen tasks. No analysis on how performance degrades with decreasing task similarity or overfitting risks
critique_points.point_011: Lack of discussion on related concurrent work. No comparison or contrast with recent multi-task supervised pre-training efforts like UnifiedSKG or T5-based models
